Product Design Personalization UX Research

Improving Food Discovery in Swiggy to Reduce Decision Fatigue

Helping users decide what to order faster through personalized meal suggestions and simplified food discovery.

The Problem

Swiggy offers thousands of restaurants and menu items in every city. While variety is valuable, it can also create decision fatigue. Many users spend several minutes browsing restaurants and menus before deciding what to order.

The abundance of choices often leads to:

  • Prolonged browsing sessions
  • Difficulty choosing meals
  • Delayed purchases
  • Users leaving the app without ordering

This phenomenon is known as choice overload, where too many options make decision-making harder instead of easier. For food delivery platforms like Swiggy, this can negatively impact conversion rates, order frequency, and overall user satisfaction.

Core Question: How might Swiggy help users decide what to order faster without reducing the variety of options available on the platform?

Evidence of the Problem

Several signals indicate decision fatigue is a real issue in food delivery apps.

App Store Reviews

Users often mention:

  • "Too many restaurants, hard to choose"
  • "I spend more time deciding than eating"
  • "Wish there was a quick pick option"

User Behavior Signals

Possible product metrics that could indicate this issue:

Session Browsing Time

Users spending long periods scrolling before ordering

Restaurant Page Visits

Users opening many restaurant pages before choosing

Menu Scroll Depth

Extensive scrolling without adding items to cart

Session Abandonment

Users leaving the app without placing an order

Industry Research

Studies on choice overload show that when users are presented with too many options, conversion rates decrease and decision time increases. Research by Sheena Iyengar demonstrates that reducing choices can actually increase purchase rates.

Understanding the Current User Journey

Below is a simplified version of how users currently order food on Swiggy:

1
Open Swiggy
User opens app feeling hungry
2
Browse restaurants
Scroll through hundreds of options
3
Scroll through cuisines
Filter by cuisine type
4
Open multiple restaurant menus
Compare options across restaurants
5
Compare items and prices
Decision fatigue sets in
6
Eventually place order or abandon
User either orders or gives up

Visual Flow Diagram

User opens app feeling hungry
↓
Scrolls through hundreds of restaurants
↓
Opens multiple restaurant menus
↓
Compares items and prices
↓
Decision fatigue sets in
↓
Eventually orders or abandons session

Key Friction Points

  • Too many restaurants to evaluate
  • No quick way to narrow down choices
  • Users must manually explore menus
  • Recommendations may not always match user intent
  • Cognitive load increases with each option viewed

Research & Discovery

Research Approach

To validate the problem and understand user behavior, the following research methods could be used:

User Interviews

Interview ~25 frequent Swiggy users across Mumbai, Bangalore, and Delhi to understand how they decide what to order.

Key questions:

  • How long do you usually take to decide what to order?
  • What makes choosing food difficult?
  • What would help you decide faster?
  • Do you often order the same items?
  • What time of day do you typically order?

Product Analytics

Analyze behavioral data such as:

  • Time spent before adding items to cart
  • Number of restaurant pages opened
  • Abandonment rate after browsing
  • Repeat order patterns
  • Peak ordering times

App Store Review Analysis

Review customer feedback related to browsing difficulty and restaurant discovery.

Competitive Analysis

Evaluate how other platforms help users discover food faster.

Competitive Analysis

Uber Eats

Personalized home feed based on past orders

DoorDash

Quick picks and trending dishes

Zomato

Curated collections like "Best Burgers Near You"

Key Insight:

Competitors simplify discovery through personalized recommendations and curated food categories, helping users decide faster without overwhelming them. Swiggy could improve its experience by reducing cognitive load and guiding users toward relevant options.

Key Insights

From research and competitive analysis, several insights emerge:

  • Users often open multiple restaurants before deciding
  • People tend to order similar food during specific times of day
  • Past orders strongly influence future food choices
  • Personalized suggestions can significantly reduce browsing time
  • Users don't necessarily want more options — they want the right options faster

Problem Framing

User Story

"As a Swiggy user, I want quick and relevant food suggestions so that I can decide what to order without spending several minutes browsing."

Success Metrics

The success of this initiative would be measured using the following metrics:

-30%
Time to Order
+15%
Order Conversion Rate
-20%
Session Abandonment
+12%
Repeat Orders

Constraints

  • Must preserve restaurant visibility and fairness
  • Should not reduce discovery of new restaurants
  • Must work with existing recommendation infrastructure
  • Should not overwhelm users with additional UI elements

Proposed Solution

A three-part solution to reduce decision fatigue while preserving choice.

1. Quick Meal Suggestions

A new section on the home screen offering instant meal recommendations based on:

  • Past order history
  • Time of day
  • User preferences
  • Popular dishes nearby

Example interface:

Hungry? Try these quick picks

🍛 Butter Chicken Combo

🍚 Biryani Special

🥗 Veg Thali

Users can order in one tap.

2. Mood-Based Food Discovery

Introduce mood-based categories that simplify browsing.

Examples:

  • Comfort Food
  • Healthy Meals
  • Quick Bites
  • Late Night Cravings

This allows users to choose a mood instead of browsing endlessly.

3. Smart Reorder

Highlight frequently ordered meals and restaurants.

Example:

You ordered this last Friday at 8 PM

Order again?

This enables fast reordering with minimal decision effort.

Implementation Strategy

Phase 1: Personalization Engine

Develop recommendation models using:

  • Past orders
  • Browsing behavior
  • Time of day
  • Location
  • User preferences

Phase 2: UI Integration

Add new sections to the home screen:

  • Quick Picks
  • Smart Reorder
  • Mood-based categories

Conduct internal usability testing.

Phase 3: Experimentation

Run A/B tests comparing current experience vs new discovery features.

  • Control Group: Existing Swiggy interface
  • Test Group: New discovery features
  • Duration: 3 weeks
  • Sample size: ~18,000 sessions per group across 5 major cities

Design Wireframes

Below are conceptual wireframes showing the proposed solution:

Quick Picks Section
Hungry? Try these quick picks
🍛
Butter Chicken Combo
Paradise Biryani • ₹280 • 25 min
Order
🍚
Chicken Biryani
Meghana Foods • ₹320 • 30 min
Order
🥗
Veg Thali
MTR • ₹180 • 20 min
Order
Mood-Based Discovery
What are you in the mood for?
🍕 Comfort Food
🥗 Healthy Meals
⚡ Quick Bites
🌙 Late Night
Smart Reorder
Your usual orders
Chicken Biryani
You ordered this last Friday at 8 PM
Reorder
Margherita Pizza
Ordered 3 times this month
Reorder

Expected Impact

User Benefits

  • Faster decision-making
  • Reduced browsing frustration
  • More relevant recommendations
  • Improved overall ordering experience

Business Benefits

  • Increased order conversion rate
  • Higher user engagement
  • Improved retention
  • Increased average order frequency

Projected Results

+15%
Order Conversion Rate
-30%
Time to Order
-20%
Session Abandonment
+12%
Repeat Order Rate

Risks & Mitigation

Risk: Over-personalization

Users may only see familiar restaurants.

Mitigation: Include "Explore New Restaurants" sections.

Risk: Recommendation inaccuracies

Poor recommendations could reduce trust.

Mitigation: Continuously retrain recommendation models using new data.

Risk: Reduced restaurant discovery

Smaller restaurants may lose visibility.

Mitigation: Blend personalized recommendations with trending and sponsored listings.

What I Would Do With Real Data

If I had access to Swiggy's internal data, I would focus on four key areas:

📊 Data Analysis

Average time to checkout, restaurant page visits per session, menu scroll depth, and cart abandonment patterns

👥 User Segmentation

New vs returning users, frequent vs occasional users, time-of-day patterns, and geographic preferences

🧪 Experiments

Test popularity-based, collaborative filtering, and hybrid recommendation models to optimize conversion

💼 Business Modeling

Impact on order frequency, customer lifetime value, restaurant partner satisfaction, and revenue per user

Key Learnings

  • Too many choices can reduce user satisfaction
  • Personalized recommendations can significantly improve conversion
  • Food discovery is as important as delivery logistics
  • Simplifying decision-making can drive both user satisfaction and revenue growth

Next Steps

  1. Build high-fidelity design prototypes in Figma
  2. Conduct usability testing with 20 users
  3. Deploy MVP recommendation system
  4. Run A/B testing for 3 weeks
  5. Iterate based on user behavior and metrics

Outcome

By helping users decide what to order faster, Swiggy can reduce decision fatigue while increasing engagement and order frequency. This initiative demonstrates how better product design and personalization can improve both user experience and business outcomes.
